name: Vegas Bajas del Guadiana Loop
name_original: Rodea la ribera del Río Guadiana — circular desde Badajoz
description: Cycle the moderate 54.7-mile Vegas Bajas del Guadiana Loop, exploring agricultural landscapes and old railway lines near Badajoz.
region_intro: Road cycling around the Guadiana offers diverse landscapes, transitioning from coastal areas to mountainous hinterlands. The region is characterized by rolling hills, river valleys, and rugged cliffs, with the Guadiana River as a central feature. Cyclists encounter Mediterranean forests, cork oak woodlands, and open plains, providing varied terrain for different skill levels. The topography includes both gentle gradients near the river and more undulating sections with significant elevation changes. Best road cycling routes around the Guadiana The most popular road cycling route is Vegas Bajas del Guadiana Loop, a 54.7 miles (88.1 km) trail that takes 4 hours 29 minutes to complete. This moderate route explores the lowlands of the Guadiana, offering expansive views. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Mérida and Esparragalejo Loop via the Guadiana River, an easy 22.5 miles (36.2 km) path. This route follows the Guadiana River, passing through historical areas and offering scenic riverside stretches. Local road cyclists also love the Mérida, Torremejía, and Alange Loop, a 43.6 miles (70.1 km) trail leading through varied terrain around historical towns, often completed in about 3 hours 22 minutes. Road cycling around the Guadiana is defined by river valleys, rolling hills, and Mediterranean forests. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy riverside rides to more challenging routes with notable climbs. The routes in Guadiana are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 50 reviews. More than 3000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Guadiana's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in the Guadiana region?
A: The Guadiana region offers a wide selection of road cycling routes, with over 130 options available. These routes cater to various skill levels, from easy riverside rides to more challenging climbs.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling in the Guadiana region?
A: Road cycling in the Guadiana region features diverse topography, including rolling hills, picturesque river valleys, and rugged cliffs. As you cycle from south to north, you'll experience a transition from coastal areas to more mountainous landscapes, with Mediterranean forests and cork oak woodlands. Routes often include both gentle gradients and significant elevation changes.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Guadiana?
A: Yes, the Guadiana region has several road cycling routes suitable for beginners. There are 28 easy routes, many of which follow the Guadiana River, offering scenic riverside stretches. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e994479106/merida-and-esparragalejo-loop-via-the-guadiana-river" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mérida and Esparragalejo Loop via the Guadiana River&lt;/a&gt;, an easy 36.2 km path that passes through historical areas.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes in the Guadiana region?
A: For those seeking a challenge, the Guadiana region offers 29 difficult road cycling routes with notable climbs and descents. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992993881/cijara-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cíjara Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt;, spanning 87.8 km with over 1200 meters of elevation gain, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992992123/orellana-la-vieja-and-castillo-de-puebla-de-alcocer-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Orellana la Vieja and Castillo de Puebla de Alcocer Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a 120.4 km route with over 1400 meters of ascent, provide a significant workout while showcasing dramatic landscapes.

Q: What natural features can I see while road cycling in Guadiana?
A: The Guadiana region is rich in natural beauty. You can encounter the dramatic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/guide/405503/attractions-around-the-guadiana" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadiana River Gorge&lt;/a&gt; with its unique geological formations, and the stunning &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7435263" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pulo do Lobo Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; (Wolf's Leap), which marks the steepest natural decline in the Guadiana River's course. The region also features extensive Mediterranean forests, cork oak woodlands, and important wetlands.

Q: Are there any historical sites or towns to visit along road cycling routes in Guadiana?
A: Yes, the region is steeped in cultural history. You can cycle past ancient archaeological sites and traditional villages. Notable historical towns include Mértola, with its medieval castle and Islamic heritage, offering panoramic views. The historical site of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/guide/405503/attractions-around-the-guadiana" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mina de São Domingos&lt;/a&gt;, a former mining complex, provides insight into industrial archaeology. Additionally, you might encounter landmarks like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3775017" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Temple of Diana (Mérida)&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3813989" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Milagros Aqueduct&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes in the Guadiana region?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes in the Guadiana region are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991600494/vegas-bajas-del-guadiana-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vegas Bajas del Guadiana Loop&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992993177/surround-the-garcia-de-sola-reservoir-circular-via-herrera-del-duque" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Surround the Garcia de Sola Reservoir — circular via Herrera del Duque&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1079679713/tablas-de-daimiel-national-park-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tablas de Daimiel National Park Loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in the Guadiana region?
A: The best time for road cycling in the Guadiana region is typically during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, the weather is milder and more pleasant, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the cooler, potentially wetter conditions of winter. This allows for comfortable riding and enjoying the vibrant landscapes.

Q: Are there family-friendly road cycling options in Guadiana?
A: Yes, the Guadiana region offers family-friendly road cycling options, particularly among its 28 easy routes. These routes often feature flatter terrain and follow scenic paths, such as those along the Guadiana River. It's advisable to check individual route details for specific suitability for children or trailers, as some paths may have varying surfaces.

Q: Is it possible to spot wildlife while road cycling in Guadiana?
A: Absolutely. The Parque Natural do Vale do Guadiana is a significant sanctuary for wildlife. While road cycling, you might spot a wide array of birdlife, including raptors and waterfowl. The region is also a habitat for endangered species such as the Iberian imperial eagle and, potentially, the Iberian lynx, especially in quieter, more remote areas.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Guadiana?
A: The Guadiana region is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 50 reviews. Road cyclists often praise the diverse landscapes, the tranquility of the region, and the variety of routes that cater to different ability levels, from easy riverside rides to challenging climbs through rolling hills and Mediterranean forests.

Q: Are there any specific long-distance road cycling routes in the Guadiana region?
A: Yes, the Grande Rota do Guadiana (GR15) is a significant route that spans approximately 65 km, connecting the coast with the mountainous hinterland and traversing diverse districts. It's known for its varied landscapes and historical, heritage, and natural features, offering a comprehensive experience of the region.
tips: Old bridge that is over the Río Gevora and from where it is not possible to drive. A cycle path appears next to it, and where it is made of iron, you can ride on it safely.
The Canal de Montijo is a water conduit that irrigates the set of crops found around and where it runs parallel to the Río Guadiana.
Spectacular church located in the old center of the city of Montijo. The space that surrounds it is always full of people and with atmosphere.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Vegas Bajas del Guadiana Loop?
faqs.answer: The loop is circular from Badajoz, making it a convenient starting point. You can typically find parking in or around Badajoz, especially near the Vía Verde sections or former railway stations, some of which offer tourist services.

faqs.question: Is the Vegas Bajas del Guadiana Loop suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Yes, parts of the loop, particularly along the Vía Verde, are largely flat with minimal elevation gain, making them suitable for cyclists of all levels. However, some segments might include unpaved sections or varied terrain, which could be more challenging. For the full 88 km loop, a moderate level of fitness is recommended.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Spring and autumn are ideal. In spring, you'll enjoy the vibrant display of fruit trees in bloom and mild temperatures. Autumn offers cooler weather and pleasant conditions. Summers can be very hot in this region, so if cycling then, it's best to go early in the morning or late in the afternoon.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this cycling loop?
faqs.answer: No, generally, you do not need any permits or have to pay entrance fees to cycle the Vegas Bajas del Guadiana Loop, as it primarily uses public greenways and roads.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the Vegas Bajas del Guadiana Loop?
faqs.answer: The terrain is largely flat, especially along the Vía Verde sections, which follow an old railway line. However, the broader area also includes sections with gentle hills and wooded pastureland. While many parts are paved, some segments, particularly on the Vía Verde, consist of compacted soil. A more robust bike than a standard road bike might be beneficial for these varied sections.

faqs.question: What can I see along the Vegas Bajas del Guadiana Loop?
faqs.answer: You'll experience picturesque scenery dominated by the fertile agricultural landscape of the Guadiana river basin, with fruit trees and various crops. Notable points of interest include the Bridge over the Gévora River, the Canal de Montijo, and the Church of Saint Peter the Apostle. You might also spot wildlife at bird viewpoints and observe storks' nests.

faqs.question: Is the Vegas Bajas del Guadiana Loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While there are no specific restrictions mentioned, dogs are generally welcome on leash on public greenways and cycling routes. Be mindful of agricultural areas and local wildlife, and always ensure your dog is under control.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of the Camino Natural del Guadiana and the Camino Natural del Corredor Cáceres - Badajoz, integrating it into wider networks like the 'Green routes' and 'EuroVelo 1'.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities or services available along the route?
faqs.answer: Some former railway stations along the Vía Verde sections may offer tourist services, including bike rentals and guided tours. The route passes through agricultural areas, so you might find small towns or villages with cafes and shops, but it's always wise to carry enough water and snacks.

faqs.question: What type of bike is best suited for this loop?
faqs.answer: Given the mix of paved roads and compacted soil sections, especially on the Vía Verde, a robust road bike, gravel bike, or even a hybrid bike would be ideal. While a standard road bike can manage, a bike with slightly wider tires would offer more comfort and stability on unpaved segments.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the Vegas Bajas del Guadiana Loop?
faqs.answer: The full loop is approximately 88 kilometers (55 miles) long. For an average cyclist, it can take around 4.5 to 5 hours of riding time, not including stops. This duration can vary based on your fitness level, the type of bike you're using, and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.

waypoints.name: Church of Saint Peter the Apostle
